title: Chronic Pain and Its Relationship to Unresolved Trauma
researcherTopic: structural dissociation, healing the fragmented self
metaDescription: Chronic pain is often the body's way of holding onto unresolved trauma. Learn how to listen to your body's signals and find a path to healing.
imageDescription: A warm, luminous image of golden light filtering through leaves, suggesting hope and gentle healing. The focus is on the interplay of light and shadow, creating a sense of peace and possibility.
htmlContent:
The pain arrives on a Tuesday, unannounced, a low thrum behind the left shoulder blade with no discernible origin. It’s not the sharp cry of a fresh injury, but the persistent hum of a signal that has been trying to get through for years, a message from a part of the body holding a story long after the mind has moved on. The body, after all, is a perfect archive; it never forgets, even when we wish it would.

The Body as a Living Archive

We tend to think of memory as a cognitive function, a library of images and narratives stored neatly in the brain. Yet, for those who have navigated complex trauma, memory is a far more visceral, embodied phenomenon, living not just in the mind but in the tissues, the fascia, the very architecture of our physical form. these are not merely random physiological events; they are often the echoes of past experiences, the body’s way of holding onto what the conscious mind cannot process. This is the literal, biological reality of how mammals store overwhelming experiences that were not allowed to complete their natural cycle of activation and release.

Understanding this shifts the entire inquiry. The question ceases to be "What is wrong with my body?" and becomes "What is my body trying to tell me?" It moves us from fighting the symptom to listening to it, approaching the pain not as an enemy but as a messenger with critical information about the parts of ourselves left behind in the name of survival. It is an invitation to turn toward the silent stories held within our own skin; learning the language of sensation is the first step in this delicate process of translation.

When Sensation Becomes a Threat Signal

For a nervous system shaped by developmental trauma, the very experience of sensation can become interpreted as a sign of imminent danger. The ordinary signals of embodiment... a quickening heart, a warmth in the chest, a subtle shift in muscular tension... Instead, they are filtered through a lens of hypervigilance, a predictive mechanism honed by a past in which survival depended on detecting threat before it became overwhelming. The brain, trying to keep us safe, creates a feedback loop where a simple physical sensation can trigger a cascade of fear, which in turn amplifies the sensation, confirming the brain’s initial hypothesis that something is terribly wrong. What we find here is the engine of so-called "medically unexplained symptoms."

The Echoes of a Dysregulated Nervous System

Chronic pain is often the most audible symptom of a nervous system that has lost its capacity for flexible self-regulation. A healthy system moves fluidly between states of activation (sympathetic) and rest (parasympathetic), a dance of mobilization and settling. For a system conditioned by complex trauma, this fluidity is lost. or it collapses into freeze or shutdown states, which can effect as numbness and depressive fatigue. Often, it oscillates wildly between these extremes, a painful pendulum with no middle ground of calm presence.

Look, I know this is heavy material. And I know there's a part of you that might be thinking "this is too much" or "I can't handle this" or "maybe I should just close this tab." That part is trying to protect you. And that's okay. But I want you to know that you can handle this. You've already handled worse. The fact that you're here, reading this, means your survival system did its job. You made it.

What we're doing here isn't about reliving the past. It's about understanding it well enough to stop being controlled by it. There's a difference between being trapped in a memory and looking at it from a safe distance. You get to choose how close you get. You get to choose when to step back. That choice - that agency - is itself a form of healing.

A simple tool for working with specific tension points is Lacrosse Ball for Trigger Points (paid link).

What we find here is why approaches focused solely on the site of the pain miss the mark. Temporary relief from rubbing a sore muscle will fade if the underlying nervous system pattern remains unaddressed. The work is not to silence the alarm but to address the fire. It is a road explored when we discuss the four primary trauma responses and their somatic signatures. The path out of pain is not through the pain itself, but through the restoration of the entire system’s regulatory capacity. For a deeper dive, one might explore resources from teachers like Kalesh, who works at the intersection of consciousness and embodiment.

Not what you expected, right?

Janina Fisher and the Fragmented Self

To truly grasp the connection between trauma and chronic pain, we must look at how overwhelming experiences affect our very sense of self, which is where the work of researchers like **Janina Fisher** becomes indispensable. Her model of structural dissociation provides a compassionate map for understanding the "fragmented self" that often emerges from developmental trauma. She posits that the personality divides into an "Apparently Normal Part" (ANP) that manages daily life, and "Emotional Parts" (EPs) that remain frozen in the time of the trauma, holding the terror, rage, and pain of those original events.

Fisher’s work encourages us to move beyond seeing ourselves as a single, unified entity and to instead approach our inner world with curiosity, as if getting to know a system of different parts. When pain flares, instead of reacting with frustration, we can learn to ask, "Which part of me is this? It is a way of offering a compassionate internal presence to the parts of us that have carried these burdens in isolation, an approach that resonates deeply with the principles of Internal Family Systems (IFS).

A body tool that some find helpful for releasing chronic tension is Acupressure Mat and Pillow Set (paid link).

The Architecture of Somatic Pain

Trauma-tethered somatic pain has a distinct architecture, built upon the scaffolding of the body’s defensive responses. The specific location and quality of the pain often provide clues to the underlying unresolved experience. For example, chronic tension in the jaw, neck, and shoulders can be the residue of a blocked impulse to cry out or fight back. The body prepared for an action that was never completed, and the energy of that preparation remains locked in the musculature as chronic tension and pain. Similarly, pain in the hips and pelvis can be related to experiences of sexual trauma or a significant sense of instability.

Working with this architecture requires a slow, patient, and titrated approach. It is not about forcing our way through the pain, which can be re-traumatizing. Instead, it is about bringing small, manageable doses of awareness to the edges of the sensation, noticing subtle shifts in temperature, texture, and pressure without judgment. It is about finding the parts of the body that feel neutral or even pleasant and learning to anchor our attention there, creating a resource of safety from which we can gently approach the more difficult areas. This process of "pendulation," moving between areas of distress and ease, is a cornerstone of somatic therapies and is essential for the delicate work of somatic reclamation.

Re-inhabiting the Body: A Path Beyond Pain

The work out of chronic, trauma-related pain is not one of excision, but of reclamation. It is not about getting rid of a "bad" part of ourselves, but about slowly and compassionately re-inhabiting the territories of the body we were forced to abandon. It turns out to be a gradual process, unfolding over a lifetime of practice. It is the practice of returning, moment by moment, to the felt sense of the body with gentle curiosity, meeting the sensations that arise... whether painful, numb, or pleasant... without a narrative, judgment, or an agenda to fix them.

I want to pause here and check in with you. How's your body doing right now? Not your mind - your body. Is there tension somewhere? A heaviness? A restlessness? Whatever you find, it's information. It's your nervous system responding to what you're reading. And that response is valid.

One thing I've learned in my own recovery is that reading about trauma can be activating. Your body doesn't always know the difference between remembering something and experiencing it. So if you need to take a break, take a break. Put your feet on the floor. Look around the room. Name five things you can see. This isn't avoidance. This is regulation. There's a difference.

Over time, something striking begins to happen. As we consistently meet the pain with this quality of non-judgmental presence, its character begins to change. The hard block of pain starts to reveal its nuances. It may transform from a sharp sensation into a dull ache, or from pressure into vibration. These are signs that the frozen energy is beginning to thaw, that the nervous system is beginning to process the stored information. The pain may not disappear, but our relationship to it is at its core transformed. It ceases to be a source of terror and becomes simply another sensation in the vast, ever-changing space of the body. It becomes part of our story, but it no longer defines it.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link).

Frequently Asked Questions

Is it possible for my chronic pain to be trauma-related even if I don't remember any specific trauma?

Absolutely. Many people with complex trauma, particularly from early childhood, do not have explicit, narrative memories of the events. The memories are stored implicitly in the body as sensory fragments, emotional reactions, and physiological patterns. Your chronic pain can be the "memory" speaking, even if your conscious mind can't access the story.

Wild, right?

Why do my doctors say there's nothing wrong when I'm in so much pain?

That becomes a common and deeply invalidating experience. Most standard medical training is not equipped to recognize or diagnose trauma-related somatic symptoms. When tests and scans show no structural damage, many practitioners conclude the issue is not "real." Your pain is very real; its source is simply in the function of your nervous system, not the structure of your tissues, which is something most medical tests are not designed to measure.

Will therapy make my pain worse?

It is possible for pain to temporarily increase as you begin to touch into long-suppressed feelings and sensations. What we find here is why it is crucial to work with a trauma-informed therapist who understands somatic processes and can guide you in a slow, titrated way. The goal is not to overwhelm the system, but to gently increase its capacity to feel, so any increase in sensation should be manageable and part of a larger healing trajectory.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link).

Can I heal this on my own?

While there are many self-regulation tools you can learn, healing developmental trauma is at the root a relational process. We were wounded in relationship, and we heal in safe relationship ~ including the safe, attuned relationship with a therapist. A skilled practitioner provides the co-regulation that your nervous system may have never received, creating the safety needed for your own system to begin to heal itself.

The body is not a machine to be fixed, but an system to be tended. The pain is not a flaw in the design, but a signal from the space. By learning to listen with compassion and curiosity, we can begin to understand its language and, in doing so, find our way back to the wholeness that has been waiting for us all along. It is the quiet, steady work of becoming intimate with the life that is breathing through us, in all its complexity and all its beauty.
